codeSnapshot.label: kl_divergence.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# PyTorch KL Divergence
# Expects log-probabilities for input, probabilities for target
# KLDivLoss computes: sum(target * (log(target) - input))

logits_student = torch.tensor([[3.0, 1.0, 0.2]])   # model output
logits_teacher = torch.tensor([[2.5, 2.0, 0.5]])   # teacher output

T = 4.0  # temperature for knowledge distillation
log_p_student = F.log_softmax(logits_student / T, dim=1)
p_teacher = F.softmax(logits_teacher / T, dim=1)

# KL(teacher || student) — minimize how much student diverges from teacher
kl_loss = nn.KLDivLoss(reduction='batchmean')
loss = kl_loss(log_p_student, p_teacher) * (T ** 2)  # rescale by T²
print(f"Distillation KL Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

# VAE: KL between posterior q(z|x) = N(μ, σ²) and prior p(z) = N(0,1)
# Analytical form: KL = -0.5 * sum(1 + log σ² - μ² - σ²)
mu = torch.tensor([0.5, -0.3, 0.8])
log_var = torch.tensor([-0.2, 0.1, -0.5])
kl_vae = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + log_var - mu**2 - torch.exp(log_var))
print(f"VAE KL regularisation: {kl_vae.item():.4f}")

eli3: KL divergence measures 'how different is distribution Q from distribution P?' It asks: if the true distribution is P, what extra surprise do you get when you use Q instead? Zero when they're identical, positive otherwise — and it's not symmetric (KL(P||Q) ≠ KL(Q||P)).

In machine learning, KL divergence is used as a loss whenever you want to train a distribution to match another. In VAEs, you push the latent distribution to stay close to a Gaussian prior. In knowledge distillation, you push the student network to match the teacher's soft predictions.

The asymmetry is important: KL(P||Q) — called 'forward KL' — is infinite if Q assigns zero probability to anything P believes possible. KL(Q||P) — 'reverse KL' — is infinite if Q assigns anything positive to regions P says are impossible. Which direction you minimise changes the solution.

whenWhy.title: VAE regularisation: latent posterior to prior
whenWhy.body: In a Variational Autoencoder, the encoder outputs μ, σ² for a Gaussian posterior q(z|x). The KL term in the ELBO is KL(q(z|x)||p(z)) = KL(N(μ,σ²)||N(0,I)) = −0.5 Σⱼ (1 + log σⱼ² − μⱼ² − σⱼ²). This regularises the latent space to be approximately Gaussian, enabling sampling at inference. The balance between reconstruction loss and KL is controlled by β (β-VAE).

whenWhy.title: Knowledge distillation: matching teacher soft labels
whenWhy.body: Hinton et al. (2015) showed that training a student to minimise KL(p_teacher||p_student) at temperature T transfers dark knowledge beyond hard labels. Soft labels encode inter-class similarities (a '3' looks a bit like an '8'). At T=4–8, the teacher distribution is smooth enough to provide useful signal. The distillation loss is KL × T² (the T² rescales gradient magnitudes to be T-independent).

whenWhy.title: Reinforcement learning: policy divergence constraint (PPO, TRPO)
whenWhy.body: TRPO constrains policy updates: E_s[KL(π_old(·|s)||π_new(·|s))] ≤ δ. This prevents catastrophic policy updates — steps too large in policy space collapse performance. PPO approximates this with a clipped surrogate objective, implicitly controlling KL. KL is also used as an early-stop criterion in PPO: if mean KL > 1.5×target, stop updating for that batch.

deepDiveIntro: KL divergence measures the extra surprise incurred by using model distribution Q when the true distribution is P. It is always nonnegative and equals zero only when the two distributions are identical. Minimising KL divergence with respect to Q when P is fixed is equivalent to minimising cross-entropy, because the entropy of P is a constant. [1]

The two orderings of KL divergence have different practical effects. Minimising forward KL forces the model to cover all regions where P has mass, since any mode of P that Q ignores contributes infinite cost — this produces mean-seeking behaviour that spreads the model across all modes. Minimising reverse KL penalises the model for placing mass where P does not, which forces it to concentrate on a single mode rather than spreading out — this is mode-seeking behaviour and is what variational inference does in practice. [2]

deepDive.heading: Definition and Properties
deepDive.body: D_KL(P||Q) = Σ P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)) = E_P[log P/Q]. Properties: (1) ≥ 0 always (Gibbs inequality: E_P[log Q/P] ≤ log E_P[Q/P] = 0 by Jensen); (2) = 0 iff P = Q a.e.; (3) Not a metric: asymmetric, no triangle inequality; (4) D_KL(P||Q) = H(P,Q) − H(P); (5) Infinite if supp(Q) ⊄ supp(P) (Q assigns 0 to something P says is possible). [1]

deepDive.heading: Analytical KL for Gaussians (VAE)
deepDive.body: For q(z|x) = N(μ, diag(σ²)) and p(z) = N(0, I) in d dimensions: D_KL(q||p) = −(d/2) + (1/2) Σⱼ (σⱼ² + μⱼ² − 1 − log σⱼ²). This closed form allows direct gradient computation w.r.t. μ and σ (or log σ) without Monte Carlo estimation. The reparameterisation trick (z = μ + σ·ε, ε~N(0,1)) enables gradients through the sampling operation. [2]
deepDive.formula: KL(N(μ,σ²)||N(0,1)) = −0.5 Σⱼ(1 + log σⱼ² − μⱼ² − σⱼ²)

deepDive.heading: Forward vs Reverse KL in Variational Inference
deepDive.body: Variational inference minimises D_KL(q||p) (reverse KL) over a family q ∈ Q. Reverse KL is mode-seeking: if p is multimodal, q concentrates on one mode (to avoid placing mass where p is small, which would be penalised). Forward KL D_KL(p||q) is mean-seeking and requires samples from p (expectation under p). Expectation-propagation (EP) minimises forward KL. The choice of direction has a large practical impact when the target distribution p is multimodal. [2]

deepDive.heading: Jensen-Shannon Divergence and GANs
deepDive.body: JS(P||Q) = 0.5 KL(P||M) + 0.5 KL(Q||M) where M = 0.5(P+Q). JSD is symmetric, bounded in [0, log 2], and is a proper metric when square-rooted. Goodfellow et al. (2014) showed that training a GAN's discriminator to optimality causes the generator to minimise JSD(p_data||p_g). When distributions have disjoint support (common in high-dimensional data), JSD = log 2 and gradient vanishes — motivating Wasserstein GAN's use of the earth mover's distance instead. [3]

youtubeLabel: KL Divergence — StatQuest with Josh Starmer
takeaways: D_KL(P||Q) = Σ P(x) log[P(x)/Q(x)] ≥ 0; zero iff P=Q. [1]
Asymmetric: forward KL is mean-seeking; reverse KL is mode-seeking. [2]
For Gaussians: KL(N(μ,σ²)||N(0,1)) = −0.5 Σ(1+log σ² − μ² − σ²) — closed form. [2]
VAEs minimise ELBO = reconstruction − β·KL; distillation uses KL to match teacher. [2]
JSD = symmetric KL variant; GAN training implicitly minimises JSD (or Wasserstein). [3]

edgeCases.q: Why does KL(P||Q) blow up when Q assigns zero probability to something P considers possible?
edgeCases.a: D_KL(P||Q) = Σ P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)). When Q(x)=0 and P(x)>0, the term is P(x)·log(∞) = ∞. This means minimising forward KL forces Q to cover all modes of P — you cannot afford to miss any region where P has mass. This 'zero-forcing' property of reverse KL (D_KL(Q||P)) is symmetric: when P(x)=0 and Q(x)>0, the term is Q(x)·log(Q(x)/0) = ∞, so Q is forced to be zero wherever P is zero (mode-seeking).

edgeCases.q: In knowledge distillation, why multiply the KL loss by T²?
edgeCases.a: With temperature T, the student minimises KL(p_teacher^T || p_student^T) where p^T ∝ exp(z/T). The gradient of this loss w.r.t. the student logit zᵢ is (p_student,i^T − p_teacher,i^T)/T. Without the T² rescaling, the gradient magnitudes are 1/T of what they'd be at T=1, making the distillation signal T times weaker than the hard-label CE loss. Multiplying by T² restores the gradient to the same scale. This is the justification from Hinton et al. (2015) for the T² factor.
